critic_raw:
The outcome is defined as 'pain reduction 30 days post-surgery,' but the hypothesis states the model predicts 'post-surgical recovery times.' Pain reduction is a surrogate outcome that is neither equivalent to recovery time nor directly measures it. The experiment cannot confirm or deny the stated hypothesis — it tests a different outcome variable entirely.

The dataset is restricted to patients aged 60 and above from a 24-month window at a single hospital. No sample size is reported. Knee replacement volumes at a typical hospital are 200-500 procedures per year, suggesting an N of 400-1000 patients. Fine-tuning ResNet-18 (11 million parameters) on this sample size for a regression task is severely underpowered; the model is likely to memorize patient-level imaging artifacts rather than learn generalizable anatomical predictors.

Preprocessing normalization is fit on the entire dataset before splitting — ResNet-18 fine-tuning parameters and image normalization statistics include test-set MRI distributions.

The CNN receives MRI scans while the XGBoost baseline explicitly does not. This is not a fair architectural comparison — it is a modality comparison. The CNN has access to imaging information unavailable to the baseline. Any gain attributed to the CNN could be entirely from imaging data access rather than deep learning over traditional regression. The correct comparison for the stated hypothesis would be CNN vs. a model that also uses image features (e.g., extracted radiomic features fed to XGBoost).

MAPE is the primary metric for predicting pain reduction. When true pain reduction values approach zero (patients with minimal post-operative pain change), MAPE becomes undefined or extremely large, destabilizing the evaluation. If pain is measured on a 0-10 scale and many patients have near-baseline scores at 30 days, MAPE will be dominated by a small number of near-zero outcomes, not by the typical prediction quality.

Surgeon ID is acknowledged as a confound but is only addressed post-hoc via sensitivity analysis. Surgeon skill is one of the strongest known drivers of orthopedic recovery outcomes — not including it as a feature in both models or stratifying the split by surgeon creates a confound that could entirely explain CNN performance differences if surgeons using specific implant types also use specific MRI protocols.
all_issues_raised: The outcome measured is 'pain reduction at 30 days' but the hypothesis claims to predict 'recovery time' — these are different endpoints and the experiment cannot test the stated hypothesis.
No sample size is reported; a 24-month single-hospital knee replacement cohort likely contains 400-1000 cases, which is severely underpowered for fine-tuning ResNet-18 on MRI regression.
Preprocessing normalization is fit on the entire dataset before splitting — MRI intensity statistics from test-set scans influence the normalization applied during training.
The CNN receives MRI scans while XGBoost does not — the comparison is modality access versus model architecture; any CNN advantage cannot be attributed to deep learning over traditional regression.

MAPE is undefined or extreme when true recovery values approach zero, which will occur for patients with minimal pain change, making the primary metric numerically unstable for a substantial portion of the test set.

Surgeon ID is mentioned as a confound but not controlled in the split strategy — surgeon skill is a dominant driver of knee replacement outcomes and its omission from stratification can produce confounded comparisons.
all_issues_adjudicated: The outcome measured is 'pain reduction at 30 days' but the hypothesis claims to predict 'recovery time' — these are different endpoints and the experiment cannot test the stated hypothesis.
No sample size is reported; a 24-month single-hospital knee replacement cohort likely contains 400-1000 cases, which is severely underpowered for fine-tuning ResNet-18 on MRI regression.
Preprocessing normalization is fit on the entire dataset before splitting — MRI intensity statistics from test-set scans influence the normalization applied during training.
The CNN receives MRI scans while XGBoost does not — the comparison is modality access versus model architecture; any CNN advantage cannot be attributed to deep learning over traditional regression.

MAPE is undefined or extreme when true recovery values approach zero, which will occur for patients with minimal pain change, making the primary metric numerically unstable for a substantial portion of the test set.

Surgeon ID is mentioned as a confound but not controlled in the split strategy — surgeon skill is a dominant driver of knee replacement outcomes and its omission from stratification can produce confounded comparisons.
